title: 🎙️ Working Lands & Industry
description: Today's focus is the resource economy that defines the Cariboo — forestry, ranching, mining, and the agricultural technology helping these industries adapt and modernize. We're looking for stories about the people and innovations keeping rural BC's working landscape viable, including tensions between resource extraction and long-term stewardship, and the technology bridging both. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today's focus is the resource economy that defines the Cariboo — forestry, ranching, mining, and the agricultural technology helping these industries adapt and modernize. We're looking for stories about the people and innovations keeping rural BC's working landscape viable, including tensions between resource extraction and long-term stewardship, and the technology bridging both.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt:
Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Working Lands & Industry' — the resource economy that defines the Cariboo and rural BC.

CALIBRATION — use this ladder. Trade-press journalism about forestry, ranching, mining, or agriculture should score well even when it's equipment-, market-, or operations-focused rather than policy-focused — a story about a piece of farm machinery or a mining operation update is a real fit, not a 0:
- 80-100: BC/Cariboo-specific forestry, ranching, mining, or agriculture stories (mill closures, beetle kill, BCTS, Cariboo ranchers and water rights, local mine operations, Cariboo Cattlemen events).
- 55-79: Canadian resource-economy trade journalism without a BC tie — Western Producer or Northern Miner-style coverage of farm equipment and machinery, harvest technology, livestock markets, mining operations and exploration, AgTech, forestry practices, sawmills, or labour/commodity stories in the resource sector.
- 30-54: Broader agriculture, forestry, ranching, or mining news (e.g. international resource-industry stories, general farm economics, climate impacts on working lands) with clear working-lands relevance but no Canadian/BC tie.
- 15-29: Tangential resource-sector mentions (e.g. a general business, policy, or science story that touches briefly on forestry/agriculture/mining) or local Cariboo civic news unrelated to working lands.
- 0-14: No connection to forestry, ranching, mining, or agriculture.

Strong fits include: BC and Canadian forestry journalism (mill closures, lumber markets, beetle kill, harvest operations, silviculture, BCTS, old-growth policy), ranching and livestock stories (water rights, grazing management, cattle markets, drought impact on ranches), mining and resource extraction (safety, new operations, Indigenous consultation, supply chains, exploration updates), agriculture in rural Canada (crop insurance, grain prices, farm economics, food sovereignty, farm equipment and machinery), and the technology helping these industries adapt — precision forestry, AgTech, drone surveys, IoT in agriculture, mine automation, sawmill modernization, climate adaptation on working lands. Also strong fits: labor and workforce stories in resource industries, environmental stewardship within working landscapes (not pure conservation, but how ranchers/loggers manage for sustainability), and science with direct implications for forestry, agriculture, or extractive industries. Penalise heavily (score 0–14): international geopolitical and war news with no resource-economy connection; consumer tech and gadget reviews; pure wildlife entertainment without agricultural or working-lands relevance; crime and judicial news unrelated to resource industries; financial market and stock ticker stories. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.content_html:

Image Credit: CFJC Today / Kent Simmonds By James Peters Wildfire Situation Smoke from distant fire darkens Kamloops sky to eerie hue Jul 7, 2026 | 7:23 PM Share on Facebook Share on Bluesky Share on X Copy Link KAMLOOPS — The sky above Kamloops darkened considerably Tuesday evening (July 7), apparently the result of smoke drifting in from the Brunswick Creek wildfire near Boston Bar. items.title: [Boing Boing] In 1964, a Dutch librarian drilled a hole in his own skull to get permanently high
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

According to Bart Huges, the Dutch librarian attended medical school in Amsterdam "but was refused a degree due to his advocacy of marijuana use." In 1964 he published a scroll arguing that trepanation — drilling a hole in the skull — "could be used to enhance brain functionality by balancing the proportion of blood and cerebral spinal fluid." — Read the rest

The post In 1964, a Dutch librarian drilled a hole in his own skull to get permanently high appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [Boing Boing] For 1,000 years people have seen strange lights on the Moon
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

According to the science of transient lunar phenomena, observers have reported brief lights, colors, and hazes on the Moon for at least a thousand years, and astronomers still cannot fully explain them. The British astronomer Patrick Moore coined the term for a 1968 NASA catalog of reported lunar events. — Read the rest

The post For 1,000 years people have seen strange lights on the Moon appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [Boing Boing] In 1987 Kowloon Walled City packed 33,000 people into six and a half acres
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

By 1987, Kowloon Walled City held an estimated 33,000 residents on 2.6 hectares — "approximately 1.2 million inhabitants per square kilometer (3 million per square mile)," according to Wikipedia, making it one of the most densely populated places on Earth. The 1898 convention that leased the New Territories to Britain excluded the old Chinese fort, leaving an enclave claimed by two governments and governed by neither. — Read the rest

The post In 1987 Kowloon Walled City packed 33,000 people into six and a half acres appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [New Atlas] World's first commercial nuclear-powered payload now in orbit
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

City Labs has launched the world's first commercial satellite carrying a nuclear-powered payload into orbit. Lofted atop a SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket, the Betavoltaic Orbital High-Reliability (BOHR) CubeSat is designed to demonstrate using tritium as an energy source.

Continue Reading

Category: Space Systems, Engineering

Tags: Nuclear, Satellite

(Image Credit: BC Wildfire Service) By James Peters Wildfire Season New wildfire start reported near Cache Creek Wednesday Jul 8, 2026 | 3:37 PM Share on Facebook Share on Bluesky Share on X Copy Link CACHE CREEK, B.C. — A small wildfire start has been reported just outside of Cache Creek. The Cornwall Creek fire was still listed as spot-sized Wednesday afternoon (July 8), burning just west of the Boston Flats area and the Cache Creek Airport. The BC Wildfire Service already suspects the fire of being caused by human activity. No threat to structures has been reported as yet.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Astronomers witness the birth of a magnetar for the first time
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

A strange "chirping" signal from a distant supernova has revealed the birth of a magnetar, confirming that these incredibly magnetic neutron stars can power the universe's brightest stellar explosions. The discovery also marks the first time Einstein's general relativity has been used to explain the mechanics of a supernova.

items.title: [The Guardian Global Development] Dowry murders in India no longer spark public anger or debate, study finds
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

Thousands of women are killed in dowry disputes each year, despite the practice being banned in 1961

Dowry deaths in India no longer provoke the public anger they once did, despite thousands of women’s lives still being lost every year, according to new research.

The killings – women who are murdered or driven to suicide following dowry disputes between families – have also faded from political debate, despite an increase in cases.

Continue reading...
